findings.summary: RC input-conditioning filters correctly detected with accurate cutoff frequency
findings.details: All 9 channels of the 9-channel Schmitt trigger design have R2(18K2)+C(100nF) RC low-pass filters correctly identified with fc=87.45 Hz (verified: 1/(2*pi*18200*100e-9) = 87.45 Hz). The analyzer correctly identified R2 as the filter resistor and excluded the upstream series resistor R1(82K) that is isolated by diode D1.

findings.summary: Subcircuit descriptions empty — Schmitt trigger input conditioning topology not classified
findings.details: The 9-Schmitt design uses 74LVC3G14 (Triple Schmitt-Trigger Inverter ICs) with per-channel R-D-RC conditioning networks. All 9 subcircuit entries have description='' — the analyzer failed to classify the topology as 'Schmitt trigger input conditioning' or any equivalent label. Given that the part name 74LVC3G14 explicitly identifies it as a Schmitt trigger and the RC filter + series diode + series resistor pattern is a textbook Schmitt trigger input conditioning circuit, this is a missed classification opportunity.

findings.summary: total_components=16 but components list has 18 entries due to multi-unit IC (74LVC3G14 U1 appears 3 times)
findings.details: The schematic statistics report total_components=16 matching unique reference count. However, the components list contains 18 entries because U1 (74LVC3G14, a 3-gate IC) is listed three times — once per gate unit. The statistic total_components=16 correctly counts physical components by unique reference designator. However, this multi-unit representation is potentially confusing: unique_parts=8 (by value grouping) and total_components=16 (unique refs) are both reported, but the number of component entries in the list (18) does not match total_components (16). This inconsistency could mislead downstream consumers of the JSON.
